headline: Majors underwater across the board while funding inverts on the heavyweights; whales mixed between exchange deposits and cold storage hoarding.
paragraphs: The regime is broken across the board. Bitcoin sits 47.5% below its 12-month high at 65,457, Ethereum 60.7% below at 1,895, and the entire cohort trades below the 200-day moving average with no golden crosses in sight. All seven majors are below their 200-day line. This is a bear regime, plain and simple, confirmed by structure rather than sentiment. The tape does show small positive daily moves, though: BTC up 0.62%, ETH up 0.94%, SOL up 0.22%, all modest and in a range-bound world. The depth of drawdown from peak is the real signal. Solana and Dogecoin have fallen hardest, down 69.3% and 75.8% respectively from the 12-month peak. Cardano has cratered furthest of all, down 82.5%, though it did not move today. This is not a correcting bull market; this is a market still pricing in a collapse from prior extremes.

Momentum is oddly split. Bitcoin and Ethereum both show MACD above signal, which would normally suggest acceleration, yet both trade below the 200-day line and both sit in the middle of their RSI ranges (BTC at 55.6, ETH at 58.6). That is classic rest within a downtrend, not reversal. RSI in the 50s means no overbought or oversold condition. Solana is different: its MACD has turned below signal line and RSI sits at 48.6, in early weakness territory. Doge has the weakest momentum by far, RSI at 35.4 with MACD still above signal, a divergence: the indicator suggests some upside while price momentum is fading and the 24-hour move was only 1.2%. The narrative is that larger caps are showing the faintest signs of steadying (higher RSI, MACD aligned) while the smaller and more volatile assets are rolling over first.

Derivatives positioning reveals the real fragmentation. Bitcoin and Ethereum funding rates have gone negative across the board: Bitcoin is at negative 0.9% annualized (minus 0.0008% per 8-hour cycle), Ethereum is far worse at negative 5.7% annualized. Negative funding means shorts are paying longs to hold; the market is overlong and liquidation risk exists on the upside if price squeezed higher, but current price action does not support a squeeze. The long-short ratios are tilted long at 1.54 for Bitcoin and 1.48 for Ethereum, expensive structures. Solana, XRP, and Doge tell a different story: they show positive funding (Solana at 2.3%, XRP at 5.5%, Doge at negative 7.8% annualized), and Doge has a jaw-dropping 5.4 long-short ratio, meaning longs outnumber shorts by more than five to one. That is not healthy crowding; it is a knife edge. Over the last 24 hours, liquidations have favored longs overwhelmingly: Bitcoin saw 15.8 million in long liquidations versus 2.3 million in shorts, Ethereum 15.3 million in longs versus 4.1 million in shorts, and Doge 2 million in longs versus 23,000 in shorts. The market is taking longs off the board, which is what you see when price is not cooperating with the prevailing bet.

Whale flows are layered and contradictory in a way that matters. The broad headline is that whales moved 48.6 million net off-exchange over the last 48 hours, and the biggest move was 102 million in stablecoin withdrawn from Binance. That is precautionary: moving dry powder off-exchange is a de-risking move. However, the same window saw 238 million in Bitcoin inflow to exchanges offset by 287 million in outflows, a net exit of 48.6 million. The three largest individual moves onto exchanges were Coinbase, MEXC, and Binance receiving between 61 and 63 million in Bitcoin each. So some whales are testing exits (moving to exchanges), while others are moving cold storage ammunition offline. The weekly trend provides context: four weeks back, net whale flows were positive 1.97 billion, then 970 million, then negative 163 million, then negative 192 million most recently. The trend is negative. Stablecoin float sits at 306 billion, down 1.98% over 30 days, which means dry powder is being deployed or spent, not accumulated.

Sentiment is pegged at fear (28 on the 0-100 index) with a 30-day range of 11 to 33. The crowd is in the lower half of its recent fear distribution, which historically tends to coincide with capitulation or shakeout phases. That creates a disconnect: the tape shows broken structure, negative funding on the heavyweights, long liquidations, and whales moving cautiously, yet the sentiment gauge is not at extremes. Spot ETF flows show Bitcoin saw a 225 million outflow on 23 July (the latest published date) against a year-to-date cumulative of 51.7 billion inflow, so the traditional-finance bid is now stepping back. Ethereum ETFs took in 26.3 million the same day, cumulative 11.3 billion, a steadier picture. The total market cap fell 0.24% over 24 hours while Bitcoin dominance sits at 56.8%, stable and centered.

Watch for two things: first, the funding flip on Bitcoin and Ethereum. If negative funding persists and the long-short ratio begins to roll over (shorts accumulate), that would signal structural weakness breaking into recognition. Second, monitor whale flows back onto exchanges over the coming week; if the net turns positive again after four weeks of outflows, that would suggest longs being tested and exit liquidity being sought. A reclaim and hold above the 200-day line by any of the majors would be a regime shift worth noting, but today that line is overhead and untested.
